past_medical_history: Alcohol use disorder, diagnosed 5 years ago, intermittent binge pattern
Hypertension, diagnosed 4 years ago, poorly controlled
Depressive disorder, managed with outpatient therapy
Remote history of deep vein thrombosis in left leg 8 years ago, completed 6-month warfarin course
family_history: Mother had a stroke at age 68
Father with coronary artery disease, stented at age 60

social_history.alcohol: Heavy episodic use, binge drinking 2-3 times per week, consumed approximately 1 bottle of wine the evening before presentation
social_history.living_situation: Lives with husband and two teenage children

neurological_exam.mental_status: Somnolent, arousable to loud voice and sternal rub. Oriented to self only when aroused. Speech is severely dysarthric with effortful, slurred output. Unable to follow two-step commands. Intermittently follows simple one-step commands. GCS 11 (E3 V3 M5).
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils 3 mm bilaterally, reactive but sluggish. Horizontal gaze-evoked nystagmus in both directions, more prominent on rightward gaze. Bilateral sixth nerve palsies with incomplete abduction bilaterally. Corneal reflexes present but diminished. Lower facial weakness is difficult to lateralize due to somnolence. Gag reflex diminished bilaterally. Unable to protrude tongue on command.
neurological_exam.motor: Withdraws all four extremities to noxious stimuli. When briefly more alert, demonstrates diffuse mild weakness approximately 4/5 bilaterally in upper and lower extremities, slightly worse on the left. Increased tone noted in bilateral lower extremities.
neurological_exam.sensory: Withdraws to pain in all four extremities. Detailed sensory examination limited by decreased level of consciousness.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Upper extremities 2+ bilaterally. Lower extremities 3+ bilaterally with bilateral upgoing plantar responses (positive Babinski signs bilaterally).
neurological_exam.coordination: Severe truncal ataxia when sat upright; unable to maintain seated position without support. Appendicular dysmetria present bilaterally on finger-to-nose when patient is cooperative, worse on the left. Heel-to-shin testing grossly dysmetric bilaterally.
neurological_exam.gait: Unable to stand or ambulate due to severe truncal ataxia and decreased consciousness.
neurological_exam.additional: NIHSS score: 14. Initial presentation at 07:00 was attributed to alcohol intoxication (BAC 2.3 per mille). Neurological status initially improved by late morning with resolution of dysarthria and ataxia, but then acutely worsened at approximately 14:00 with progressive somnolence, new diplopia, dysphagia, and return of severe dysarthria and ataxia.

patient.chief_complaint: Unable to be awakened after drinking, initial improvement then acute neurological deterioration
patient.history_present_illness: Ms. Katarina Novak is a 50-year-old right-handed woman brought to the emergency department at approximately 07:00 by her husband, who reports he was unable to awaken her that morning after she had consumed approximately one bottle of wine the prior evening. On initial evaluation, the patient was somnolent with moderate dysarthria, horizontal gaze-directed nystagmus, marked truncal ataxia, and bilateral upper and lower extremity incoordination. Given her known history of episodic heavy alcohol use, the clinical team initially attributed her symptoms to acute alcohol intoxication. Serum ethanol level returned at 2.3 per mille, consistent with significant intoxication. Forced diuresis was initiated, and the patient was placed on hourly neurological monitoring. By approximately 10:30, her clinical status appeared to improve substantially: she became more alert, her dysarthria resolved, and her ataxia improved significantly. However, at approximately 14:00, the nursing staff documented acute deterioration with rapidly worsening somnolence, new complaints of diplopia, difficulty swallowing, and recurrence of severe horizontal nystagmus and dysarthria. The patient's husband, who had remained at bedside, confirmed she had been conversant and sitting up comfortably just 30 minutes prior. There was no witnessed seizure activity, no head trauma, and no febrile illness. She had not vomited and there was no headache reported during her lucid interval. The pattern of initial improvement followed by sudden worsening raised concern for an evolving posterior circulation event rather than simple alcohol intoxication, prompting urgent re-evaluation. Of note, the family history is significant for early cerebrovascular disease in the mother, and the patient has a remote history of DVT.

findings.location: Bilateral cerebellar hemispheres and dorsal pons

signal_characteristics.DWI: Subtle, questionable hyperintensity in the dorsal pons and left cerebellar hemisphere; may represent artifact versus early restricted diffusion
signal_characteristics.ADC: Equivocal; no definite hypointensity to confirm restricted diffusion
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: No definite hyperintensity in the posterior fossa; normal-appearing brainstem and cerebellum
signal_characteristics.T2: Normal signal intensity in brainstem and cerebellum

additional_observations: Mild mucosal thickening in bilateral maxillary sinuses, incidental
MRA of intracranial vessels: bilateral vertebral arteries appear patent, basilar artery signal is diminished in the mid-to-distal segments with possible flow-related artifact versus stenosis
No evidence of venous sinus thrombosis on MRV sequences
mri.impression: 1. No definite acute infarction identified. Subtle DWI signal in the dorsal pons is equivocal and may represent artifact; clinical correlation recommended. 2. Diminished flow signal in mid-to-distal basilar artery on MRA — may represent slow flow, stenosis, or artifact. Further evaluation with CT angiography recommended if clinical concern for posterior circulation pathology. 3. No intracranial hemorrhage.

differential_by_imaging.key_features: Subtle DWI signal in pons, diminished basilar MRA signal; DWI may be falsely negative within first 6-12 hours of posterior circulation stroke
No definite structural abnormality; clinical symptoms could represent toxic-metabolic effect
No characteristic periaqueductal or mammillary body signal abnormality

recommended_actions: CT angiography of head and neck to evaluate basilar artery patency
Repeat MRI in 12-24 hours if clinical suspicion persists and initial imaging equivocal
Consider transcranial Doppler for hemodynamic assessment of posterior circulation

findings: Left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage criteria (SV1 + RV5 = 37 mm)
T-wave inversions in V1-V2, likely strain pattern versus normal variant
ecg.interpretation: Normal sinus rhythm with left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage criteria. No arrhythmia or acute ischemic changes identified.
ecg.clinical_correlation: No cardioembolic source identified on ECG. The LVH is consistent with the patient's history of hypertension. Normal sinus rhythm makes atrial fibrillation-related cardioembolism less likely, raising the question of cryptogenic stroke if ischemic etiology is confirmed.

CBC.clinical_significance: Mild macrocytosis, consistent with chronic alcohol use and/or folate deficiency
Low-normal to mildly decreased, may reflect nutritional deficiency in setting of alcohol use

BMP.clinical_significance: Mildly low, may be related to poor oral intake and diuresis

Toxicology and Other.clinical_significance: Significantly elevated, consistent with acute intoxication; however, neurological symptoms persisted and worsened beyond expected timeline for metabolic clearance
Elevated, consistent with chronic alcohol use and/or liver enzyme induction
Mildly elevated, consistent with alcohol-related hepatocellular injury
Mildly low, common in chronic alcohol use; may lower seizure threshold

Lipid Panel.clinical_significance: Elevated, above target for patient with vascular risk factors
Mildly elevated, may be related to alcohol use and metabolic factors

labs.interpretation: Laboratory results demonstrate significantly elevated blood ethanol level consistent with acute intoxication, along with markers of chronic alcohol use (elevated GGT, macrocytosis, mildly elevated AST, low magnesium). Mild stress hyperglycemia and mild hypokalemia are present. Coagulation studies are normal. Lipid panel shows suboptimal values. No evidence of acute cardiac injury. The ethanol level initially supported a diagnosis of intoxication, but the clinical trajectory of improvement followed by deterioration is atypical for simple alcohol intoxication.

signal_characteristics.CTA: Complete occlusion of the mid-to-distal basilar artery with abrupt cutoff. Proximal basilar artery is patent with a thrombus interface visible at approximately the mid-basilar level. No antegrade contrast filling beyond the occlusion site.

Left vertebral artery is dominant and patent. Right vertebral artery is hypoplastic with reduced caliber but patent throughout its course. No dissection flap or intramural hematoma identified in either vertebral artery.
Bilateral posterior communicating arteries are small-caliber, with limited collateral supply to the posterior circulation. Anterior circulation patent bilaterally.

additional_observations: Anterior circulation is fully patent bilaterally including ICAs and MCAs
Mild atherosclerotic calcification at the bilateral carotid bifurcations without hemodynamically significant stenosis
Dense basilar sign noted on non-contrast CT corresponding to the occlusion site
output.impression: 1. Acute mid-to-distal basilar artery occlusion confirmed on CTA. This is a neurovascular emergency requiring immediate consideration of endovascular thrombectomy. 2. Limited posterior communicating artery collateral supply increases the risk of devastating brainstem and cerebellar infarction. 3. No vertebral artery dissection identified. 4. Anterior circulation patent.

differential_by_imaging.key_features: Complete cutoff of contrast at mid-basilar level with dense basilar sign on NCCT

differential_by_imaging.diagnosis: Basilar artery atherosclerotic stenosis with superimposed thrombosis
differential_by_imaging.key_features: May have underlying intracranial atherosclerotic disease as the etiology

recommended_actions: Emergent neurointerventional consultation for basilar artery thrombectomy
Consider IV thrombolysis as bridge to thrombectomy if within time window
Continuous neurological monitoring with preparations for airway protection

findings.location: Bilateral cerebellar hemispheres (left greater than right), dorsal pons, and left lateral medulla
findings.size: Left cerebellar lesion approximately 3.2 x 2.1 cm, right cerebellar lesion approximately 1.5 x 1.0 cm, pontine lesion approximately 1.8 x 1.2 cm

signal_characteristics.DWI: Markedly hyperintense restricted diffusion in bilateral cerebellar hemispheres (left > right), dorsal pons, and left lateral medulla
signal_characteristics.ADC: Corresponding hypointensity confirming true restricted diffusion in all affected areas
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Hyperintense signal in bilateral cerebellar hemispheres and dorsal pons, consistent with established infarction
signal_characteristics.T2: Hyperintense signal corresponding to DWI abnormalities

findings.mass_effect: Mild effacement of the fourth ventricle from bilateral cerebellar edema. No obstructive hydrocephalus at this time.
findings.borders: Lesions conform to posterior circulation vascular territories

additional_observations: New findings compared to initial MRI obtained 8 hours earlier — initial study was falsely negative in the hyperacute period
MRA now demonstrates absent flow signal in mid-to-distal basilar artery, confirming persistent occlusion
Fourth ventricle is partially effaced; close monitoring for obstructive hydrocephalus recommended
output.impression: 1. Multifocal acute posterior circulation infarctions involving bilateral cerebellar hemispheres, dorsal pons, and left lateral medulla, in the territory of the basilar artery. 2. Persistent basilar artery occlusion on MRA. 3. Mild mass effect on the fourth ventricle from cerebellar edema without obstructive hydrocephalus. 4. Initial MRI was falsely negative, consistent with known limitations of DWI in hyperacute posterior circulation stroke.

differential_by_imaging.diagnosis: Acute posterior circulation infarction due to basilar artery occlusion
differential_by_imaging.key_features: Multifocal DWI restriction in basilar artery territory with confirmed basilar occlusion on MRA

recommended_actions: Emergent thrombectomy if not yet performed
Serial neuroimaging to monitor for hemorrhagic transformation and hydrocephalus
Neurosurgical consultation for possible posterior fossa decompression if mass effect progresses
ICU-level monitoring with frequent neurological assessments

Transthoracic Echocardiogram.value: Bubble study positive for right-to-left shunt with Valsalva
Transthoracic Echocardiogram.clinical_significance: PFO identified with right-to-left shunting — potential conduit for paradoxical embolism given history of prior DVT

output.interpretation: Transthoracic echocardiogram with bubble study reveals normal left ventricular systolic function, mild concentric LVH consistent with hypertension, and a patent foramen ovale (PFO) with right-to-left shunting on Valsalva maneuver. The PFO finding is clinically significant in the context of this patient's prior history of deep vein thrombosis and current posterior circulation stroke of uncertain etiology, as it raises the possibility of paradoxical embolism as the stroke mechanism. TEE recommended for further characterization of PFO size and atrial septal anatomy.
abnormal_values_summary: Patent foramen ovale with right-to-left shunt on Valsalva

findings: Normal sinus rhythm maintained throughout 48-hour monitoring period
No episodes of atrial fibrillation or atrial flutter detected
No significant pauses (longest pause 1.4 seconds during sleep)
Rare premature atrial complexes (28 in 48 hours, <1% burden)
Rare premature ventricular complexes (15 in 48 hours, <1% burden)
No sustained or non-sustained supraventricular or ventricular tachycardia
output.interpretation: Forty-eight hour Holter monitoring reveals normal sinus rhythm throughout with no episodes of atrial fibrillation, atrial flutter, or other significant arrhythmia. Low burden of ectopy. No arrhythmic source for cardioembolism identified.
output.clinical_correlation: Negative prolonged cardiac monitoring makes paroxysmal atrial fibrillation less likely as the stroke etiology. In the absence of an identifiable cardiac arrhythmia, consider alternative embolic sources including paradoxical embolism (given PFO and prior DVT history), intracranial atherosclerotic disease, or vertebrobasilar dissection.

Hypercoagulability Panel.clinical_significance: Heterozygous Factor V Leiden mutation identified; confers increased risk of venous thromboembolism and may have contributed to prior DVT
Mild hyperhomocysteinemia, which is an independent risk factor for both arterial and venous thrombosis; may be related to folate/B12 deficiency from chronic alcohol use

output.interpretation: Hypercoagulability panel reveals heterozygous Factor V Leiden mutation and mild hyperhomocysteinemia. Factor V Leiden increases venous thrombotic risk and, in the setting of a PFO, supports the mechanism of paradoxical embolism as a potential etiology for this basilar artery occlusion. Hyperhomocysteinemia is an additional prothrombotic risk factor that may be partially reversible with folate and B12 supplementation. No antiphospholipid syndrome markers identified.

output.query: basilar artery occlusion thrombectomy outcomes treatment guidelines

results.title: Endovascular Therapy for Acute Basilar-Artery Occlusion (ATTENTION Trial)
results.authors: Tao C, Nogueira RG, et al.
results.key_finding: Endovascular thrombectomy within 12 hours of estimated occlusion time significantly improved functional outcomes compared to medical management alone in Chinese patients with acute basilar artery occlusion (mRS 0-3 at 90 days: 46% vs 23%).

results.title: Endovascular Therapy for Basilar-Artery Occlusion (BAOCHE Trial)
results.key_finding: Thrombectomy within 6-24 hours of estimated onset improved functional outcome in basilar artery occlusion patients selected by advanced imaging (mRS 0-3: 44% vs 25%).

results.title: Basilar Artery Occlusion: Clinical and Radiographic Predictors of Outcome
results.authors: Mattle HP, Arnold M, et al.
results.key_finding: Mortality in untreated basilar artery occlusion approaches 85-95%. Early recanalization is the strongest predictor of favorable outcome.

output.summary: Basilar artery occlusion is a neurovascular emergency with extremely high mortality if untreated. Recent landmark trials (ATTENTION, BAOCHE) have demonstrated clear benefit of endovascular thrombectomy within 12-24 hours of symptom onset, with improved rates of functional independence. Patient selection using advanced imaging (CTA, CTP, or MRI) is critical. IV thrombolysis may be used as a bridge to thrombectomy. The fluctuating or stuttering symptom course seen in this case is characteristic of basilar artery occlusion and should not be mistaken for benign conditions.

interactions: Sertraline: SSRIs may increase bleeding risk due to impaired platelet aggregation. Consider risk-benefit ratio in the context of acute stroke.
contraindications: Recent (within 3 months) intracranial or intraspinal surgery or serious head trauma
Presence of intracranial conditions that may increase risk of bleeding
Current use of direct oral anticoagulants with elevated drug levels
warnings: Blood ethanol level was significantly elevated earlier; however, alcohol intoxication is not an absolute contraindication to IV tPA
Ensure current BP is below 185/110 mmHg before administration
Monitor closely for orolingual angioedema, especially in patients on ACE inhibitors (patient is on amlodipine, a CCB, not an ACEi)
alternatives: Tenecteplase 0.25 mg/kg IV bolus — single bolus administration, increasingly used in bridging-to-thrombectomy setting
Direct endovascular thrombectomy without IV thrombolysis if presentation is beyond IV tPA window

findings.location: Bilateral vertebral arteries and transcranial Doppler of basilar artery

signal_characteristics.Basilar_artery_TCD: High pulsatility index (PI = 1.8) with markedly reduced mean flow velocity (12 cm/s) in the basilar artery, consistent with high-grade stenosis or occlusion

additional_observations: Bilateral common carotid arteries demonstrate normal flow velocities and waveforms
Bilateral internal carotid arteries are patent without hemodynamically significant stenosis
Right vertebral artery is hypoplastic but patent
Left vertebral artery is dominant with normal flow
Transcranial Doppler of basilar artery demonstrates severely abnormal hemodynamics consistent with distal occlusion
output.impression: 1. Transcranial Doppler reveals high pulsatility index and markedly reduced flow velocity in the basilar artery, highly suggestive of basilar artery occlusion. 2. Normal bilateral carotid arteries. 3. Hypoplastic right vertebral artery. These findings are consistent with CTA findings and support the diagnosis of acute basilar artery occlusion.
recommended_actions: Correlate with CTA findings for thrombectomy planning
Serial TCD may be used to monitor for recanalization post-treatment

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Acute ischemic stroke due to basilar artery occlusion, posterior circulation

differential.key_distinguishing: BAC of 2.3 per mille initially supported this diagnosis; however, the clinical trajectory of improvement followed by acute worsening is inconsistent with simple intoxication, which follows a monotonic improvement curve as alcohol is metabolized

Can present with ataxia, oculomotor abnormalities, and altered consciousness in alcoholic patients, but MRI would show characteristic periaqueductal and mammillary body signal changes, which are absent; the acute fluctuating course with focal brainstem signs favors vascular etiology
Can present with vertigo, dysarthria, ataxia, and decreased consciousness, but typically in younger patients with migraine history, symptoms fully reversible, and normal imaging
Would present more subacutely with fever, CSF pleocytosis, and MRI showing brainstem signal abnormality without vascular territory restriction pattern
Can occur in alcoholic patients but requires rapid sodium correction (not present), has a different temporal profile, and shows characteristic pontine T2 signal on MRI

optimal_actions.action: Perform initial neurological assessment including GCS, focused neurological examination, and assess for posterior circulation symptoms (ataxia, nystagmus, dysarthria, cranial nerve palsies)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: GCS 11, somnolence, dysarthria, horizontal nystagmus, truncal ataxia, bilateral Babinski signs

optimal_actions.action: Obtain stat labs including CBC, BMP, coagulation studies, ethanol level, liver function, and toxicology screen
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Elevated BAC, macrocytosis, elevated GGT — supporting alcohol use but not explaining the full clinical picture

optimal_actions.action: Obtain emergent brain MRI with DWI and MRA to evaluate for posterior circulation stroke
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Initial MRI may be equivocal in hyperacute posterior circulation stroke; diminished basilar MRA signal is a red flag

optimal_actions.action: Obtain 12-lead ECG to evaluate for cardioembolic source
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal sinus rhythm — no atrial fibrillation identified, making cryptogenic stroke mechanism more likely

optimal_actions.action: Recognize that the fluctuating course (improvement then acute worsening) is a red flag for basilar artery occlusion and obtain CT angiography of head and neck urgently
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Mid-to-distal basilar artery occlusion on CTA confirming the diagnosis

optimal_actions.action: Consult neurointerventional radiology for emergent basilar artery thrombectomy
Consider IV thrombolysis (alteplase or tenecteplase) as bridge to thrombectomy, verifying no absolute contraindications
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No absolute contraindications; SSRI use is a relative concern for increased bleeding but not a contraindication

optimal_actions.action: Admit to neurological ICU with continuous monitoring and preparation for possible airway protection given brainstem involvement
Administer empiric IV thiamine 500 mg before glucose-containing fluids given alcohol use history
Order echocardiogram with bubble study to evaluate for PFO/cardioembolic source
optimal_actions.expected_finding: PFO with right-to-left shunt identified — raises possibility of paradoxical embolism

optimal_actions.action: Order hypercoagulability workup given history of prior DVT and possible paradoxical embolism
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Factor V Leiden heterozygous mutation and hyperhomocysteinemia identified

optimal_actions.action: Initiate anticoagulation for secondary stroke prevention once acute phase stable, particularly given PFO with DVT history and thrombophilia

critical_actions: Recognize that the pattern of initial improvement followed by acute worsening is inconsistent with alcohol intoxication and warrants urgent vascular imaging
Obtain CT angiography to confirm basilar artery occlusion despite equivocal initial MRI
Emergently consult for endovascular thrombectomy — basilar artery occlusion carries >85% mortality if untreated
Consider IV thrombolysis as bridge to thrombectomy
Monitor in ICU with preparedness for airway protection due to brainstem involvement
Administer thiamine before glucose-containing fluids in an alcoholic patient to prevent precipitation of Wernicke encephalopathy
contraindicated_actions: Attributing all symptoms to alcohol intoxication and discharging without vascular imaging, especially after the secondary deterioration
Delaying vascular imaging because initial MRI was equivocal
Administering IV glucose-containing fluids before thiamine in an alcoholic patient
Aggressively lowering blood pressure below 140/90 in the setting of acute basilar artery occlusion (risks worsening brainstem ischemia)
Performing lumbar puncture in the presence of posterior fossa mass effect from cerebellar edema (risk of tonsillar herniation)
Discharging the patient after initial improvement without explaining the fluctuating clinical course
key_reasoning_points: The initial presentation with somnolence, dysarthria, nystagmus, and ataxia in a patient with elevated BAC is a classic mimic scenario — alcohol intoxication can mask or mimic posterior circulation stroke symptoms

The fluctuating course (improvement then acute deterioration) is a hallmark of basilar artery occlusion, as the stuttering collateral supply alternately compensates and fails; this pattern should never be attributed to simple alcohol intoxication

DWI-negative MRI does not exclude posterior circulation stroke in the hyperacute phase; the posterior fossa is particularly susceptible to false-negative DWI due to susceptibility artifacts and small vessel territory involvement
Diminished basilar artery signal on MRA, even when read as possibly artifactual, should prompt urgent CTA when clinical suspicion exists
Transcranial Doppler showing high pulsatility index with low flow velocity in the basilar artery is a key bedside finding that can be obtained rapidly and supports the diagnosis before CTA
The combination of PFO, prior DVT, and Factor V Leiden suggests paradoxical embolism as a potential stroke mechanism in this patient without atrial fibrillation
Basilar artery occlusion is a time-sensitive emergency with mortality approaching 85-95% without recanalization; recent trials (ATTENTION, BAOCHE) demonstrate clear benefit of thrombectomy

metadata.difficulty_description: Diagnostic puzzle — initial presentation mimics alcohol intoxication with elevated BAC, initial MRI is equivocal/falsely negative for posterior circulation stroke, and the critical diagnostic clue (fluctuating course and basilar artery occlusion) only emerges on follow-up evaluation and CTA. Multiple red herrings from alcohol-related lab abnormalities.
clinical_pearls: Posterior circulation strokes are missed more frequently than anterior circulation strokes because their symptoms (vertigo, ataxia, diplopia, dysarthria) overlap with common benign conditions and intoxication
DWI sensitivity for posterior circulation stroke in the first 24 hours is only 72-80%, compared to >95% for anterior circulation stroke
The 'stuttering' or fluctuating course in basilar artery occlusion results from intermittent collateral compensation and should trigger immediate vascular imaging
Approximately 25% of the general population has a PFO; its significance increases dramatically when combined with venous thrombosis history and thrombophilia
